questions.question: The minimum number of comparisons required to sort 5 elements is ______
questions.to_find: Minimum number of comparisons needed to sort 5 elements in the worst case

understand.plain: We need to find the fewest comparisons any sorting algorithm must make in the worst case to sort 5 elements. This is an information-theoretic lower bound question. The decision-tree lower bound gives ceil(log_2(5!)) as the minimum comparisons needed. However, the question is asking for the minimum worst-case comparisons of the best known algorithm for 5 elements, which is 7.

keywords.explain: Any comparison-based sort of n elements needs at least ceil(log_2(n!)) comparisons in the worst case.
keywords.example: For n=5: 5! = 120, log_2(120) = 6.9, so ceil = 7 comparisons minimum.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where each internal node is a comparison and each leaf is a permutation. The tree must have at least n! leaves, so height >= ceil(log_2(n!)).
keywords.example: For 5 elements, we need at least 7 levels in the decision tree.

understand.visual_alt: Decision tree for sorting 5 elements requires at least 7 levels since 2^7 = 128 >= 5! = 120 but 2^6 = 64 < 120.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of comparisons to sort 5 elements.

terms.example: 5 distinct elements in some unknown order

terms.meaning: Total number of possible orderings of 5 elements
terms.example: The decision tree must have at least 120 leaves
terms.connects: Lower bound on tree height = ceil(log_2(120))

terms.meaning: Information-theoretic lower bound on comparisons for n elements
terms.connects: Gives the minimum number of comparisons = 7

given.plan: Use the decision tree lower bound: any comparison-based sorting algorithm needs at least ceil(log_2(n!)) comparisons. For n=5, compute ceil(log_2(5!)) = ceil(log_2(120)) = 7. Verify that 7 comparisons are actually achievable (Ford-Johnson / merge-insertion sort achieves this).

steps.title: Compute the lower bound
steps.apply: n = 5, so n! = 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 120. log_2(120) = log(120)/log(2) = 2.079/0.301 = 6.906. ceil(6.906) = 7. So we need at least 7 comparisons.
steps.note: 2^6 = 64 < 120 means 6 comparisons cannot distinguish all 120 orderings; 2^7 = 128 >= 120 so 7 suffices in theory.

steps.title: Verify achievability
steps.apply: The Ford-Johnson (merge-insertion) sort algorithm achieves exactly 7 comparisons in the worst case for n=5. Therefore the minimum is exactly 7.
steps.note: For n=1,2,3,4,5 the minimums are 0,1,3,5,7 comparisons respectively.

solution.result: The minimum number of comparisons required to sort 5 elements is 7.

questions.formula_note: Decision tree lower bound: ceil(log_2(n!)) gives the minimum comparisons for comparison-based sorting. Ford-Johnson algorithm achieves this bound for small n.

questions.question: Give an optimal algorithm in pseudo-code for sorting a sequence of n numbers which has only k distinct numbers (k is not known a Priori). Give a brief analysis for the time-complexity of your algorithm.
questions.to_find: An optimal algorithm to sort n numbers with only k distinct values (k unknown), and its time complexity

understand.plain: We have n numbers but only k unique values among them (k is not known in advance). We want to sort these numbers efficiently. Since k is unknown, we can use a hash map (or BST) to count occurrences of each distinct element as we scan through, then sort just the k distinct values and expand them back. The time complexity is O(n + k log k), which simplifies to O(n log k) since k <= n.

keywords.explain: Among n numbers, only k are unique. Many numbers repeat.
A dictionary mapping each distinct value to how many times it appears.

understand.visual_alt: Phase 1: scan n elements to build frequency map (O(n)). Phase 2: sort k distinct keys (O(k log k)). Phase 3: output each key freq[key] times (O(n)). Total: O(n + k log k).

given.aim: Sort n numbers having only k distinct values (k unknown) in optimal time.

terms.meaning: Total number of elements in the sequence
Number of distinct values (unknown, k <= n)
terms.connects: Sorting only k keys costs O(k log k)

terms.meaning: A hash map storing count of each distinct element
terms.example: freq[x] = number of times x appears
terms.connects: Built in O(n); allows reconstruction in O(n)

given.plan: 1) Scan all n numbers and build a hash map freq[x] counting occurrences. 2) Extract the k distinct keys and sort them using any O(k log k) sort. 3) Output each key freq[key] times to get the sorted sequence. Total time: O(n + k log k) = O(n log k) since k <= n.

steps.title: Pseudo-code of algorithm
steps.apply:
Algorithm SortWithKDistinct(A[1..n]):
  freq = empty hash map
  for i = 1 to n do
    freq[A[i]] = freq[A[i]] + 1   // count frequencies, O(n)
  keys = list of all keys in freq  // k distinct keys
  Sort(keys)                        // sort k keys: O(k log k)
  idx = 1
  for each x in keys (sorted) do
    for j = 1 to freq[x] do
      A[idx] = x
      idx = idx + 1               // write back: O(n)
  return A
steps.note: If a hash map is unavailable, a balanced BST (e.g. AVL tree) can be used: insert/lookup in O(log k) each, total O(n log k) for all n insertions.

steps.title: Time complexity analysis
steps.formula_raw: T(n, k) = O(n) + O(k log k) + O(n) = O(n + k log k)
steps.apply: Since k <= n: k log k <= n log n, but also k log k <= n log k. The dominant term depends on k. If k << n then O(n) dominates; if k = Theta(n) then O(n log n). In general the complexity is O(n + k log k). This is optimal because: reading n elements takes Omega(n), and sorting k distinct values needs Omega(k log k) by comparison lower bound. So the algorithm is optimal.
steps.note: This is better than O(n log n) when k is small. For k = O(1), it reduces to O(n).

solution.result: Optimal algorithm uses a frequency map + sort of k keys. Time complexity: O(n + k log k), which is O(n log k) in the worst case.

questions.formula_note: Frequency counting reduces sorting n elements with k distinct values to sorting only k elements: O(n + k log k). Uses comparison-based lower bound Omega(k log k) for the sorting step.

questions.question:
Following algorithm(s) can be used to sort n numbers in the range [1..n^k] in O(n) time:
A. Heap sort
B. Quick sort
C. Merge sort
D. Radix sort
questions.to_find: Which sorting algorithm(s) can sort n numbers in the range [1..n^k] in O(n) time

understand.plain: We have n numbers, each in the range 1 to n^k (where k is a constant). Comparison-based sorts like heap sort, quick sort, and merge sort all take Omega(n log n) in the worst case, so they cannot be O(n). Radix sort is a non-comparison sort that can exploit the structure of the numbers. If numbers are in [1..n^k], each number has at most k*log_2(n) bits, and Radix sort with base n takes k passes of O(n), giving O(kn) = O(n) for constant k.

keywords.explain: Any algorithm that only uses comparisons between elements needs Omega(n log n) time in the worst case.
keywords.example: Heap sort, merge sort, quick sort all take Omega(n log n) worst case.

keywords.explain: A non-comparison sort that processes digits of numbers from least to most significant. For numbers in [1..n^k], use base n: each number has k 'digits', and each pass is O(n). Total: O(kn) = O(n) for constant k.
keywords.example: Numbers in [1..n^2]: represent each in base n as 2-digit number, sort in 2 passes of O(n) each.

understand.visual_alt: Heap sort, Quick sort, Merge sort: Omega(n log n) - cannot achieve O(n). Radix sort with base n: k passes each O(n), total O(kn) = O(n) for constant k.

given.aim: Identify which sorting algorithm can sort n numbers in [1..n^k] in O(n) time.

terms.meaning: Numbers are bounded by n^k where k is a constant
terms.example: If n=100, k=2, numbers are in [1..10000]
terms.connects: In base n, each number needs exactly k digits, enabling O(n) Radix sort

terms.meaning: Any comparison sort needs Omega(n log n)
terms.example: Heap sort worst case: O(n log n)
terms.connects: Rules out options A, B, C for O(n) goal

terms.meaning: Sorting with n as the base, each pass is a stable sort into n buckets
terms.example: For n=100, base-100 sort of numbers in [1..10000] needs 2 passes
terms.connects: k passes x O(n) per pass = O(kn) = O(n) for constant k

given.plan: Eliminate comparison-based sorts (A, B, C) using the Omega(n log n) lower bound. For Radix sort (D): represent each number in base n; since numbers <= n^k, each has at most k digits. Radix sort does k passes, each O(n + n) = O(n). Total: O(kn) = O(n) for constant k.

steps.title: Eliminate comparison-based sorts
steps.formula_raw: Omega(n log n) lower bound for comparison sorts
steps.apply: Heap sort (A): O(n log n) worst case - NOT O(n). Quick sort (B): O(n^2) worst case, O(n log n) average - NOT O(n). Merge sort (C): O(n log n) always - NOT O(n). All three are comparison-based and cannot beat Omega(n log n).
steps.note: No comparison-based algorithm can sort in O(n) in general.

steps.title: Verify Radix sort achieves O(n)
steps.formula_raw: T = k * O(n) = O(kn) = O(n) for constant k
steps.apply: Numbers in [1..n^k] have value < n^k. Represent each in base n: it needs exactly k digits (0 to n-1 each). Radix sort processes k digit positions, each using counting sort over n buckets in O(n) time. Total time = k * O(n) = O(n) since k is a constant. Therefore Radix sort achieves O(n).
steps.note: This is a classic result: Radix sort with base n and k digit positions runs in O(kn) = O(n) for constant k.

solution.result: Only D (Radix sort) can sort n numbers in [1..n^k] in O(n) time.

questions.formula_note: Radix sort with base n on numbers in [1..n^k]: k passes x O(n) per pass = O(kn) = O(n) for constant k. Comparison-based sorts are all Omega(n log n) and cannot achieve O(n).

questions.question:
Consider the following sequence of numbers:
92, 37, 52, 12, 11, 25
Use Bubble sort to arrange the sequence in ascending order. Give the sequence at the end of each of the first five passes.
questions.to_find: Sequence after each of the first 5 passes of Bubble sort on [92, 37, 52, 12, 11, 25]

understand.plain: Bubble sort repeatedly scans the array and swaps adjacent elements if they are out of order. In each pass, the largest unsorted element 'bubbles up' to its correct position at the end. After pass i, the i largest elements are in their correct positions at the end of the array.

keywords.explain: One complete scan from left to right, swapping adjacent out-of-order pairs. After each pass, the maximum of the unsorted portion is placed correctly.
Bubble sort only swaps elements that are next to each other in the array.
keywords.example: Compare A[0] with A[1]: if A[0]>A[1] swap. Then compare A[1] with A[2], etc.

understand.visual_alt: Table showing the array state after each of 5 bubble sort passes. Purple cells indicate elements in their final sorted positions. After pass 5 the array is fully sorted: 11,12,25,37,52,92.

given.aim: Apply 5 passes of Bubble sort to [92, 37, 52, 12, 11, 25] and record state after each pass.

terms.example: 6 elements to sort in ascending order

terms.meaning: One left-to-right scan with adjacent swaps
terms.connects: After each pass, next largest is in place

terms.meaning: Elements at the end that are already in final position
terms.example: After pass 1: [92] at end is sorted
terms.connects: Each pass shrinks the unsorted prefix by 1

given.plan: Execute Bubble sort pass by pass. In each pass scan from left, compare adjacent pairs, swap if left > right. Track the sequence after each pass.

steps.title: Pass 1 on [92, 37, 52, 12, 11, 25]
steps.note: 92 (the largest) bubbles all the way to the end in one pass.

steps.title: Pass 4 on [12, 11, 25 | 37, 52, 92]
steps.note: Array is now fully sorted after pass 4.

steps.title: Pass 5 on [11, 12, 25 | 37, 52, 92]
steps.apply:
Compare 11 and 12: 11<12, no swap
Compare 12 and 25: 12<25, no swap
After Pass 5: 11, 12, 25, 37, 52, 92 (unchanged)
steps.note: No swaps in pass 5 confirms the array was already sorted after pass 4.

questions.formula_note: Bubble sort: in each pass the current maximum bubbles to its correct position. After i passes, the i largest elements are in their final positions.

questions.question:
A two-dimensional array A[1..n][1..n] of integers is partially sorted if A[i][j] < A[i][j+1] and A[i][j] < A[i+1][j] for all 1 <= i, j <= n-1.

a. The smallest item in the array is at A[i][j] where i = ___ and j = ___.

b. The smallest item is deleted. Complete the following procedure to insert item x (which is guaranteed to be smaller than any item in the last row or column) still keeping A partially sorted.

procedure insert(x: integer);
var i, j: integer;
begin
  A[1][1] := x; i := 1; j := 1;
  repeat
    [S]
  until A[i][j] = x
end;

A. S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end
B. S: if A[i][j+1] > A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end
C. S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end
D. S: if A[i][j+1] > A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end
questions.answer: A. S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end
questions.to_find: Location of smallest element and correct procedure to re-insert an element maintaining partial sortedness

understand.plain: A 2D array is partially sorted if each element is smaller than its right neighbor and its bottom neighbor. This means A[1][1] is the global minimum (since everything to its right and below it is larger). When we remove the minimum and insert a new x (smaller than all last-row and last-column elements), we place x at A[1][1] and then 'trickle it down' by swapping it with the smaller of its right and bottom neighbors, until it reaches a stable position.

keywords.explain: A[i][j] < A[i][j+1] and A[i][j] < A[i+1][j] for all valid i,j. Every element is less than its right and bottom neighbors.
keywords.example: Like a min-heap generalized to 2D. A[1][1] is always the global minimum.

keywords.explain: After placing x at top-left, swap it with the smaller of its right/bottom neighbor, moving x towards lower-right until it is no longer larger than any neighbor.
keywords.example: x=5 at A[1][1], right=3, bottom=7: swap with right (3<7), x moves to A[1][2].

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 320 200" width="320" height="200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="20" y="20" width="60" height="50" class="hi"/><text x="50" y="52" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">A[1][1]</text><text x="50" y="68" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">min</text><rect x="85" y="20" width="60" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="115" y="52" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">A[1][2]</text><rect x="150" y="20" width="60" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="180" y="52" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">...</text><rect x="20" y="75" width="60" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="50" y="107" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">A[2][1]</text><rect x="85" y="75" width="60" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="115" y="107" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">A[2][2]</text><rect x="150" y="75" width="60" height="50" class="bg"/><text x="180" y="107" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">...</text><text x="230" y="52" class="lbl ink">Since</text><text x="230" y="72" class="sub ink">A[i][j] <</text><text x="230" y="90" class="sub ink">A[i][j+1]</text><text x="230" y="110" class="sub ink">A[i][j] <</text><text x="230" y="128" class="sub ink">A[i+1][j]</text><text x="20" y="165" class="sub ink">A[1][1] is the global minimum</text><text x="20" y="185" class="sub mut">(i=1, j=1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 2D grid where A[1][1] (top-left) is highlighted as the global minimum. Every element is smaller than its right neighbor and bottom neighbor, so the minimum must be at position (1,1).

given.aim: Find minimum location in partially sorted 2D array; complete insert procedure that maintains partial sortedness.

terms.meaning: Partial sortedness condition: each element is smaller than right and bottom neighbors
terms.connects: Part (a): smallest element is at i=1, j=1

terms.meaning: Place x at A[1][1], then trickle it down by swapping with smaller neighbor
terms.example: x at (i,j): if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j], swap with right and move right; else swap with bottom and move down
terms.connects: Maintains property that each position is less than right and bottom neighbors

terms.meaning: To restore partial sort, x should move toward the smaller of the two candidates
terms.example: If right=3 and bottom=7, swap x with right (3) since we want the smaller value to come back to (i,j)
terms.connects: Ensures A[i][j] < A[i][j+1] and A[i][j] < A[i+1][j] after the swap

given.plan: Part (a): By the partial sort property, A[1][1] < all elements in row 1 and column 1, and transitively < all elements, so min is at i=1, j=1. Part (b): Place x at A[1][1]. The repair strategy is: compare A[i][j+1] and A[i+1][j] (the right and bottom neighbors). Swap x with the smaller one (to put the smaller value back at current position), and move in that direction. Repeat until x is in the right place.

steps.title: Part (a): Location of minimum
steps.apply: By the partial sort condition, A[1][1] < A[1][j] for all j (by transitivity along row 1) and A[1][1] < A[i][1] for all i (by transitivity along column 1). Therefore A[1][1] < A[i][j] for all i, j. The smallest item is at i=1, j=1.
steps.note: This is analogous to how the root of a min-heap is the global minimum.

steps.title: Part (b): Choosing the correct repair step S
steps.apply: After placing x at A[1][1], we need to move x to a valid position. At each step, we compare A[i][j+1] (right) and A[i+1][j] (below). We want to put the smaller of these two back at position (i,j) to restore the partial sort property. So if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j]: swap A[i][j] with A[i][j+1] and move j := j+1. Else: swap A[i][j] with A[i+1][j] and move i := i+1. This matches option A.
steps.note: We swap with the SMALLER neighbor (not larger) because we need A[i][j] to be less than both neighbors after the swap. Option B swaps with the larger neighbor which would violate the condition.

steps.title: Verify option A maintains partial sortedness
steps.apply: Suppose A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j]. After swapping A[i][j] (=x) with A[i][j+1]: A[i][j] = A[i][j+1] (old) which is the smaller of the two neighbors, so A[i][j] < A[i+1][j] (satisfied). The condition A[i][j] < A[i][j+1] (new) will be maintained as x trickles further right. This is consistent with option A.
steps.note: The algorithm terminates when x is not larger than either neighbor or when x reaches the boundary of the array.

solution.result: Part (a): Smallest item is at i=1, j=1. Part (b): Option A is correct.

questions.formula_note: Partially sorted 2D array is analogous to a 2D min-heap. Insert by placing at (1,1) and trickling down by swapping with the smaller of right/bottom neighbors, similar to heapify-down in a standard min-heap.
